results.possibleIdentity: Roberts Blossom (1924–2011), often referred to as Bob Blossom or Robert Blossom, was an American multimedia artist, filmmaker, poet, and actor who was a significant figure in the New York avant-garde scene during the 1960s and 1970s. He is most noted for his 'Filmstage' productions, a series of experiments beginning in the early 1960s that integrated live theatrical performance with projected film, exploring the relationship between consciousness and corporeality. Blossom was a frequent collaborator with the Judson Dance Theater, notably working with choreographer Beverly Schmidt for 'Judson Concert No. 10' at the Gramercy Arts Theater. He was also associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement and the circle of artists around Andy Warhol and Jonas Mekas. In addition to his multimedia work, he was a poet whose work appeared in publications like 'Blue Beat' (1964) and 'Locus Solus.' While he later gained mainstream fame as a character actor in films such as 'Home Alone' and 'Close Encounters of the Third Kind,' his historical identity as an artist is defined by his 'expanded cinema' and intermedia innovations during the late 1960s.

results.possibleIdentity: Jon Hassell (1937–2021) was an American trumpet player and composer best known for pioneering the "Fourth World" music concept, which he defined as a "unified primitive/futurist sound" blending traditional ethnic music with modern electronic techniques. Active since the late 1960s, he studied serial composition and electronics under Karlheinz Stockhausen in Cologne before returning to the U.S. in 1967. In the late 1960s, he became a central figure in the minimalist movement, performing on the landmark 1968 recording of Terry Riley's "In C" and joining La Monte Young's Theatre of Eternal Music. In the early 1970s, he began an intensive study of Indian classical music under Pandit Pran Nath, which profoundly influenced his unique, vocal-like trumpet style. Hassell was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization; he contributed a piece to an E.A.T. show in New York that was built with assistance from Robert Moog, and his lobby piece "MAP" (which used hand-held playback heads over large sheets of magnetic tape) was featured in an E.A.T. exhibit at the University at Buffalo in 1974. His work in this period often crossed into the visual and sculptural arts, as seen in his "invisible sonic sculpture" piece "Solid State" (1969) and his "Landmusic" series of environmental sound proposals. He gained wider fame through his 1980 collaboration with Brian Eno, "Fourth World, Vol. 1: Possible Musics," and subsequently worked with artists such as Talking Heads, Peter Gabriel, and David Sylvian.

results.possibleIdentity: Joel B. Heathcote (1939–2024) was an American lighting artist and designer active from the late 1960s through the early 21st century. A 1961 graduate of Dartmouth College, he was known for his work in multi-media and technological arts. In the late 1960s and early 1970s, he was involved with "The World Game," a multi-media event and project conceived by Buckminster Fuller, serving as a Lighting Artist and Designer for the production. This work aligned with the goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which fostered collaborations between artists and engineers during that period. Throughout his career, Heathcote operated Limitless Light Studios in San Diego, California, specializing in the design and installation of fiber optic "night sky" star ceilings and high-tech LED and laser lighting systems. He also maintained a residence in Rumney, New Hampshire, and was a long-time member and editor for the Casque & Gauntlet senior society at Dartmouth, where he was affectionately known as "Merlin."
